title: Chapter 5: Bodega Run
content: The orange juice situation had become critical.
Maya stood in front of the open fridge like she was mourning it. "There's nothing. There's literally nothing in here except sriracha and a cucumber that's been through some things."

Chloe, curled on the couch cushion with the indentation that was now permanently her shape, hummed a low, agreeable note. She didn't need orange juice. Chloe didn't need anything except maybe a second cushion and for time to stop passing.
"Elif's on night shift until six," Maya said, closing the fridge with her hip. "And the delivery app has a fifteen-dollar minimum now. Fifteen! For orange juice!"

From the kitchen corner, Dave was eating cereal. Standing up. Eyes closed. The spoon moved from bowl to mouth with the mechanical rhythm of a factory line that had been running too long. His hair stood in seventeen directions. A milk droplet rolled down his chin and he didn't notice.
"Dave," Maya said.
"Mm."
"Dave, when did you last sleep?"
He chewed. Considered. Kept chewing. "What day is it?"
"Oh my God."
"It's fine," he said, eyes still closed. "I'll sleep after this bowl."
He'd said that three bowls ago.
"I could go," Dave offered, and the spoon missed his mouth entirely, clattering against his cheek. He didn't flinch. "I can totally go."
"You're not going anywhere. You're one mild breeze away from collapsing."
"I'm fine," he said again, and then slowly, gracefully, leaned against the wall and stayed there, vertical only through friction.
Maya looked at Chloe.
Chloe looked at Maya.
The bodega was one block away. One. Block.
"No," Maya said.
Chloe hummed.
"Absolutely not. You can't — you don't —" Maya gestured at all of Chloe. "You're a hundred and fifty pounds of capybara!"
Chloe hummed again, lower. She knew what she was. She also knew that Maya had classes at ten and hadn't had vitamin C in four days and the grocery delivery slot wasn't until Thursday.
Maya pressed her palms to her eyes. "One block."
One block.
"Okay. *Okay.*"

The coat was Dave's. Dave was six-two and built like someone who'd been assembled from spare parts in a hurry, which meant his parka could shelter a family of four. Maya wrestled Chloe into it with the determined focus of someone packing a sleeping bag.
"Arms — no, your other — *there*."

Chloe's front legs went through the sleeves. The coat swallowed her. Dave's parka on a capybara looked like a very short, very calm person in an extremely puffy coat. Which was, Maya reasoned, essentially fine.

The scarf came next. Maya wound it high, covering everything below Chloe's eyes. Then the beanie — pulled low over Chloe's round ears until only her dark eyes and the bridge of her snout were visible.
"If anyone asks, you're short."
Chloe blinked.
"Don't make eye contact. Don't hum. Walk fast."
Maya held the door open. Chloe looked at the hallway. The stairs. The street beyond.
She did not want to go outside.

She had not been outside — not for *work*, not for a purpose, not for anything that wasn't commuting or crying or both — in longer than she could remember. Her old life had been outside-adjacent at best. Subway to office, office to subway, subway to apartment. The outside was a thing between places. A hallway with weather.
Now she was going to walk one block for orange juice as a capybara in a parka.
*Sure.*
*Fine.*
*This is fine.*
February hit her like a slap from someone she'd been ignoring.

The cold was *sharp*. Not the ambient apartment chill she'd been adjusting to — this was a thing with edges. It found the gap between scarf and beanie and slid in like a whisper. Her paws, despite the coat's coverage, registered every crack in the concrete. Every ridge. Every spot where the sidewalk had buckled from tree roots and frost heave.

She'd walked this sidewalk before. Hundreds of times. In heels, in flats, in the boots she'd bought because the ad said "effortless" and they'd given her blisters so bad she'd cried in a Duane Reade. She'd walked it with her phone up, headphones in, constructing emails in her head, composing tweets she'd never post, replaying conversations she'd handled wrong.
She'd never *felt* it.

A car idled at the light. Exhaust curled from the tailpipe, pale and chemical, and Chloe's nose twitched. Two blocks down, the halal cart was out — the guy with the white cap who always played Amr Diab from a Bluetooth speaker. The smell hit her like a warm hand: lamb, onion, the char of something hitting metal. Her stomach didn't growl (capybaras didn't do that, she'd learned) but something in her chest expanded.

Someone's dryer vent puffed lint-scented steam from a basement window. A pigeon landed three feet away, regarded her with one orange eye, and departed. A woman across the street argued with her phone in a language Chloe couldn't identify but absolutely felt.
*Life.*
It was everywhere. It had always been everywhere. She'd just been too busy having a panic attack about a pitch deck to notice.
Maya walked beside her, hands in her jacket, radiating the specific tension of someone managing a situation. Chloe kept her eyes forward. Don't make eye contact. Don't hum. Walk fast.
Her paws moved. Left, right, left, right. The cold air rushed over the exposed bridge of her snout and it felt —
*Good?*
It felt good.

The bodega was orange. Not painted orange — but the light inside, the forty years of fluorescent tubes and warm beer coolers and dusty bulbs over the chip rack, had turned the air itself a soft, amber orange. Like being inside a memory of a kitchen.
Mo looked up from behind the counter.

Mo was approximately four hundred years old and had run this bodega since the neighborhood was a different neighborhood, which it had been three times over. He'd seen everything. He'd seen rent-controlled artists and landlord specials and a guy try to return a bag of ice because it was "too cold." He had a face like a topographic map and eyes that had stopped being surprised around 1987.
He looked at Chloe.
Very short person in enormous coat. Parka, scarf, beanie pulled low. Brown eyes. Rounded shape. Standing very, very still.
"The usual?"
He said it from twenty years of instinct. The reflex of a man who'd been asking people what they wanted since before Chloe was born.
Chloe froze.

Not a visible freeze — she'd already been still. But something inside her went rigid, the way it used to when someone in a meeting said "and Chloe, what are your thoughts on the timeline?" and she had to construct a person in real-time who had thoughts on the timeline.
*The usual.*

She didn't have a usual. She'd never been in this bodega. She'd been in the Duane Reade on the corner, the one that closed, the one where she'd cried about the boots. Mo didn't know her. Mo was just — being Mo. Being Brooklyn. Being the guy who asked "the usual?" because asking "the usual?" was easier than asking "what do you want?" and people liked being recognized even when they weren't being recognized, even when it was just a reflex, just a bodega guy's version of small talk, just —
Mo waited.

No follow-up question. No quirked eyebrow. No "hey, you look kinda—" Nothing. He just waited. Like a man who'd waited for ten thousand customers to figure out what they wanted and would wait for ten thousand more.
Chloe reached a paw — a paw, a leg, a foot, whatever — out from under the coat. Set a twenty on the counter.

Mo took it. Opened the register. Made change. Pushed it back across the counter. Pulled a bag of oranges from the produce crate by the door — not a request, not a question, just a motion, a flow — and set it beside the change.
Still hadn't looked up.
Chloe pushed the change back. Touched the oranges. Looked at Mo.
"Good?" Mo said.
Chloe hummed.
Mo didn't flinch. Just nodded. "The Navarros get the same ones. Good batch."
Brooklyn was a miracle.

Outside, the cold again. Sharper now, or maybe she was just more awake. The bag of oranges hung from her mouth through the scarf gap, which was undignified but functional. Maya walked on her left, close, watching the sidewalk ahead with the focus of a bodyguard.
They'd made it twenty feet when the dog appeared.

Off-leash. A pit mix, brindle, barrel-chested. Moving with the loose, easy confidence of a dog who'd never been told he couldn't go somewhere. He came around the corner of a parked van and stopped. Ears up. Nose working.
Chloe's body went still.

Not the old stillness — not the *freeze response*, not the shutdown that used to lock her in elevator foyers and bathroom stalls, not the thing where her chest got tight and her vision narrowed and she became a point of awareness the size of a pinhead, spiraling.
This was different.

This was something ancient. Something that lived in a part of her brain that hadn't changed when everything else did — or maybe it had always been there, and the anxiety had just been louder. A calm so deep it felt like sinking into warm water. Her heart rate didn't spike. Her breathing didn't change. She was just *present*, completely, the way she'd never been present in any meeting or on any sidewalk in her old life.
The dog approached. Close. Sniffed through the gap in the coat, the scarf, all of Maya's careful disguise. His wet nose pushed into fabric and found fur.
His tail wagged.
Not the uncertain wag of a dog deciding whether to be friendly. The full, loose, whole-body wag of a dog who had found something wonderful and was absolutely certain about it.
And then he lay down.
Right there on the sidewalk. Next to Chloe. Belly on concrete, head on paws, tail still going. Settled like he'd found home.
"BRUH." The dog's owner appeared — a guy in a Knicks jersey, phone still in hand, mouth open. "He NEVER does that. Never. I've had him six years — he doesn't even lie down at the *dog park*."

Chloe hummed. Low, resonant, the hum that had been coming more naturally since she'd stopped fighting it. The calm hum. The everything-is-fine hum. The I-am-a-large-docile-herbivore-and-nothing-can-hurt-me hum.
The dog's ears swiveled. His eyes half-closed.
"Yo," the guy said, stepping closer, "is that a—"
Maya grabbed Chloe's paw — the one still inside the coat sleeve — and walked.
Fast.
"Time to go. Thank you. Great dog. Bye."
The guy's voice followed them: "Wait, was that a — hello?"

Chloe let herself be led. The oranges swung gently from her mouth. Her paws found the cracks in the sidewalk again, every one of them. The cold air rushed over her snout and her fur and the parts of her that were, for the first time in her whole life, not clenched against the world.
The apartment door closed behind them and the warmth hit like a quilt.
Maya took the oranges. Put them on the counter. Stood there for a moment with both hands on the bag, breathing.
"Okay," she said. "Okay. That happened."
Chloe stood in the middle of the living room, still wearing the parka, the scarf, the beanie. Dave was asleep against the wall, bowl balanced on his chest, rising and falling.
Chloe's heart rate had never spiked.

Not when Mo looked at her. Not when the dog approached. Not when the guy started to figure it out. Not once. She could feel it — or rather, she could feel the absence of what used to be there. The tightness. The adrenaline. The constant, humming alarm of a life spent bracing for impact.

She'd had panic attacks on this block. Actual, real, chest-crushing panic attacks. On this exact sidewalk. She'd sat on the curb outside the Duane Reade (closed now, a vape shop) and breathed into a paper bag and texted her therapist and felt, in the core of her being, that she would never be okay, that this was just what being alive was, a series of emergencies strung together with coffee.

Now she was a capybara in a borrowed parka, and a dog had lain down beside her on the sidewalk, and a bodega owner had sold her oranges without asking a single question that mattered, and the cold had felt good.
She pushed the beanie off with a paw. Shook out her ears. Let the scarf fall.

Maya was juicing an orange — just one, right there, cutting it open and squeezing it into a glass with her bare hands like a person who had been pushed to the absolute limit of acceptable behavior and was now operating past it.
"Here," Maya said, sliding the glass across the counter.
Chloe looked at it.
Looked at Maya.
Pressed her snout to the glass and drank.
It was the best orange juice she'd ever had.
Outside, the city kept going. Halal carts and dryer vents and dogs and people and Mo, always Mo, behind the counter, not looking up. Brooklyn, accommodating the absurd since 1634.
Chloe hummed.
It sounded like thanks.
